name: Python
creator: Guido van Rossum
description: Python is a versatile, user-friendly language known for clear syntax and a huge ecosystem of libraries.
usedFor: Web development, automation, data analysis, scripting, machine learning, APIs.
funFact: Python is named after the British comedy group Monty Python.

topics.title: Variables and Data Types
topics.explanation: Variables store data values in Python. Types (int, float, str, etc.) are assigned dynamically, so you don't specify them explicitly.

keyTerms.definition: A named container for storing data.
Kind of value, e.g. int, float, str, bool.

topics.starterCode:
city = 
zip_code = 
print(city, zip_code)
commonMistakes: Using variables before assigning a value.
topics.challenge: Assign your favorite color and number to two variables and print them.
topics.challengeHint: Assign a string and an int to two new variables.
topics.terminalNote: Run with python lesson.py or python3 lesson.py

topics.title: Lists
topics.explanation: A list is an ordered collection of elements in Python. Lists can hold elements of different types, and can be changed after creation.
topics.starterCode:
nums = [3, 6, 9]
# Print the last number
commonMistakes: Using parentheses or braces instead of brackets.
topics.challenge: Add a new item 'grape' to the fruits list, then print the full list.
topics.terminalNote: Print statements let you inspect list values.

topics.title: Loops
topics.explanation: Loops help you repeat code. The 'for' loop iterates over items in a sequence, while 'while' repeats while a condition is true.

keyTerms.definition: Repeatedly executes code for each item in a collection.
Repeats code while a condition remains True.

topics.starterCode:
numbers = [2, 4, 6]
# Loop through and print each number
commonMistakes: Using tabs and spaces inconsistently in indentation.
topics.challenge: Print only odd numbers from 1 to 10 using a loop.
topics.challengeHint: Use range and step or check n % 2 == 1.
topics.terminalNote: Use python to run and view output lines.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Functions let you reuse code by encapsulating a block of commands as a callable routine. Use 'def' to create one.

keyTerms.definition: Python keyword to define a function.

topics.starterCode:
def greet(name):
    # Print Hello and the name
topics.challenge: Write a function that returns double the input.
topics.terminalNote: Define, then call; nothing executes until called.

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.explanation: A dictionary stores key-value pairs. Keys can be strings, numbers or tuples; values can be anything. Dictionaries are mutable.
topics.starterCode:
scores = {"A": 85}
# Add a key 'B' with 70
topics.challenge: Print all dictionary keys.
topics.terminalNote: Output will look like dict_keys([...]).

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: Classes define the structure for objects and can include attributes and methods. Use 'class' and always include self as the first argument to methods.

keyTerms.definition: A function defined inside a class.

topics.challenge: Add a honk method that returns "Honk!" and print it.
topics.challengeHint: Return a string from an instance method.
topics.terminalNote: Call methods with instance.method().

topics.title: File Input/Output
topics.explanation: Use open(), read(), write() and 'with' statements for reading and writing files safely.

keyTerms.definition: Context manager for safer file handling.

topics.starterCode:
with open("task.txt", "w") as f:
    # write something
commonMistakes: Opening with wrong mode ('r' for reading does not create the file).
topics.challenge: Append a line to a file, then read all lines and print.
topics.terminalNote: File output persists after script ends.

topics.title: Modules
topics.explanation: Python modules let you organize your code. Use 'import' to bring in modules from the standard library or third-party packages.

keyTerms.definition: A file containing Python code (functions, variables, classes).

topics.starterCode: # Import math and print square root of 49
commonMistakes: Trying to use a module without first importing it.
topics.challenge: Use the sys module to print the version of Python.
topics.terminalNote: Install third-party modules via pip.

topics.title: Exception Handling
topics.explanation: Python handles errors with try/except blocks. This keeps your program from stopping unexpectedly.

keyTerms.definition: Error that can be caught at runtime.

topics.challenge: Handle division by zero with ZeroDivisionError.
topics.terminalNote: Errors will print only when exceptions occur.

topics.title: APIs and HTTP Requests
topics.explanation: Python can fetch data from the internet using modules like 'requests'. API stands for Application Programming Interface.
topics.starterCode: # Use requests to get https://api.github.com and print JSON keys
topics.challenge: Print the value of the 'current_user_url' key from the response JSON.
topics.terminalNote: Install requests before running: pip install requests

name: JavaScript
creator: Brendan Eich
description: JavaScript is the most widely used language for interactive and dynamic web content, both in browsers and on servers (Node.js).
usedFor: Web apps, scripting, automation, servers, games.
funFact: Despite the name, JavaScript is not related to Java.

topics.title: Variables
topics.explanation: Variables store values in JavaScript. Use let and const for modern, safer code. They are dynamically typed.
topics.challenge: Create a variable for your age and one for your favorite language, print both.
topics.challengeHint: Use let and const as needed.
topics.terminalNote: Print output with node lesson.js

topics.title: Arrays
topics.explanation: An array is a list of items. JavaScript arrays can change size and hold values of any type.

keyTerms.definition: An ordered, indexable collection of items.

topics.starterCode:
const letters = ["a", "b", "c"];
// Print the second element
commonMistakes: Using 1 as the first index (should be 0).
topics.challenge: Add 'd' to the end and print full array.
topics.terminalNote: Arrays in JS can grow in size.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Functions help organize repeating logic. Use function declarations or arrow functions for concise syntax.
topics.starterCode:
const double = n => ;
console.log(double(10));
commonMistakes: Not using return for non-void arrow functions.
topics.challenge: Write a function triple(n) that returns n*3.
topics.terminalNote: Define, then call your function to see output.

topics.title: DOM Manipulation
topics.explanation: The Document Object Model (DOM) is an object representation of your HTML. Use querySelector or getElementById to select elements.

keyTerms.definition: API representing the structure of the webpage.

topics.starterCode: // Select and print the tag name of <body>
commonMistakes: Trying to run DOM code in Node.js.
topics.challenge: Print the text content of the first <h1>.
topics.terminalNote: DOM code requires a browser context.

topics.title: Events
topics.explanation: Events are how JS responds to user actions. Use addEventListener to run code when, for example, a button is clicked.
topics.starterCode: // Log a message when user clicks anywhere on body
commonMistakes: Forgetting to pass a function to the listener.
topics.challenge: Alert 'Thanks!' when a <button> is clicked.
topics.terminalNote: DOM events only work in browsers.

topics.title: Promises
topics.explanation: A Promise is an object representing the result of an asynchronous operation. Use .then() and .catch() to respond.

keyTerms.definition: Represents future completion or rejection of an async operation.

topics.starterCode: // Make a Promise resolving to your name
topics.challenge: Create a Promise that rejects and log the error.
topics.terminalNote: Promise code runs after the call stack clears.

topics.title: Async / Await
topics.explanation: async/await lets you write async code that looks synchronous. await only works inside functions marked async.

keyTerms.definition: Declares a function that returns a promise.

commonMistakes: Using await outside of async function.
Omitting to call the async function.
topics.challenge: Await a Promise that resolves after 200ms and print a message.
topics.terminalNote: Output will appear after a delay.

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: Classes are templates for creating objects with data (properties) and methods. Introduced in ES6.

keyTerms.definition: A syntax for defining object structure and behavior.

topics.challenge: Add a method author() that returns 'Unknown'.
topics.terminalNote: Construct with new and call methods.

topics.title: Modules
topics.explanation: Split code into modules for clarity. Use export and import statements (supported natively in modern browsers and via Node's ESM).

keyTerms.definition: A separate file or chunk of reusable code.

codeExample: export function add(x, y) { return x + y; }
topics.starterCode:
// In math.js export a subtract function
// In main.js import and use subtract
solution: export function subtract(a, b) { return a - b; }
commonMistakes: Not using the .js extension in imports for ESM.
Trying ESM in Node without .mjs extension or configuration.
topics.challenge: Export a constant and import it for printing.
topics.terminalNote: Node: consider using --experimental-modules for ESM.

topics.title: Fetch API
topics.explanation: Fetch API lets you request resources over HTTP in browser JS. It returns a Promise.
topics.starterCode: // Fetch https://api.github.com and print the JSON
commonMistakes: Forgetting .then() after fetch() or misusing async/await.
topics.challenge: Print the value of the 'current_user_url' property from GitHub API root.
topics.terminalNote: Works in browsers and node-fetch.

name: TypeScript
creator: Anders Hejlsberg
description: TypeScript adds static typing and advanced tooling to JavaScript, helping to avoid bugs and improve maintainability.
usedFor: Large scale web apps, Node.js, serverless development.
funFact: TypeScript is used to build the VSCode editor.

topics.title: Typed Variables
topics.explanation: Declare a variable's type with a type annotation. The compiler checks value compatibility.
topics.starterCode:
let age: number = ;
console.log(age);
commonMistakes: Forgetting the colon between variable and type.
topics.challenge: Declare a variable isReady of type boolean with value true.
topics.terminalNote: TypeScript will error on invalid assignments.

topics.title: Types and Interfaces
topics.explanation: Interfaces define an object's shape at compile time. Types can also alias unions, primitives, and more.
topics.starterCode:
type Id = string;
const uid: Id = ;
topics.challenge: Create an interface Account with number and owner (string)
topics.challengeHint: Use colon and semicolon for interface.
challengeSolution: interface Account { number: number; owner: string; }
topics.terminalNote: Interfaces checked only during compilation.

topics.title: Typed Arrays
topics.explanation: Specify a type for array elements. TypeScript ensures only correct types are stored.

keyTerms.definition: Array of type T, e.g., string[], number[].

topics.starterCode:
let nums: number[] = ;
nums = [1, 2, 3];
commonMistakes: Mixing types in a typed array.
topics.challenge: Create a boolean array of [true, false].
topics.terminalNote: Check array element type in editor/tooling.

topics.title: Functions with Types
topics.explanation: All function arguments and return types can be typed in TypeScript. Omitting them leads to inference.
topics.starterCode: function greet(name: string): string { return ; }
solution: function greet(name: string): string { return "Hi " + name; }
commonMistakes: Not specifying return type when it's not obvious.
topics.challenge: Write a function that takes a string[] and returns the first element.
challengeSolution: function first(xs: string[]): string { return xs[0]; }
topics.terminalNote: Compiler warns for type mismatches.

topics.title: Object Types
topics.explanation: You can specify object types inline or with interfaces. All properties must be present and types must match.
codeExample: let person: {name: string, age: number} = { name: "Tina", age: 33 };
topics.starterCode: let car: {make: string; year: number} = ;
solution: let car: {make: string; year: number} = {make: "Ford", year: 2020};
commonMistakes: Property missing, or wrong type for property.
topics.challenge: Make an object with 'title' (string) and 'pages' (number).
challengeSolution: let book: {title: string; pages: number} = {title: "Book", pages: 100};
topics.terminalNote: Object types are structural; order unimportant.

topics.title: Classes and Access Modifiers
topics.explanation: TypeScript classes can declare public/private/protected methods and properties, helping encapsulation.
codeExample: constructor(public name: string, secret: string) { this.secret = secret; }
solution: constructor(x: number, y: number) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
commonMistakes: Trying to access private properties outside the class.
topics.challenge: Define a class with a protected method sayHello.
topics.terminalNote: Classes compile to JS constructor functions.

topics.title: Generics
topics.explanation: Generics enable flexible, type-safe code that can operate on many types, via angle bracket syntax.

keyTerms.definition: A type parameter for generic structures.

codeExample: function id<T>(x: T): T { return x; }
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call function with an array.
topics.challenge: Make a generic Pair<T> interface (first, second).
challengeSolution: interface Pair<T> { first: T; second: T; }
topics.terminalNote: Generics greatly aid reusability.

topics.title: Enums
topics.explanation: Enums provide named constants, either numeric or string. Useful for fixed value sets.
topics.starterCode:
enum Direction { Up, Down }
let d = Direction.Up;
commonMistakes: Assuming enum names are strings (they are numbers by default).
topics.challenge: Print string name of a Color enum value.
topics.terminalNote: Enums appear as objects in JS output.

topics.title: Type Assertions
topics.explanation: Type assertions override TypeScript's type inference. Only affects compile time, not JS runtime.

keyTerms.definition: New type treat variable as (value as Type)

topics.starterCode:
let n: any = 123;
// Cast to number and log toFixed(2)
commonMistakes: Thinking this changes runtime behavior, it does not.
topics.challenge: Assert unknown to string and log length
topics.terminalNote: Assertions help TypeScript, not JavaScript.

name: C
creator: Dennis Ritchie
description: C is a foundational high-performance language giving close control over memory and the system.
usedFor: Operating systems, embedded devices, performance-critical software.
funFact: The original UNIX operating system was written in C.

topics.title: Variables and Types
topics.explanation: Variables in C must declare their type at definition time. Common types include int, float, double, char.
topics.starterCode:
float pi = ;
printf("%f\n", pi);
topics.challenge: Declare a double called e and print it.
topics.terminalNote: Compile first, then run the output file.

topics.title: Arrays
topics.explanation: Arrays let you store multiple values of the same type in a contiguous block of memory.
topics.starterCode:
int nums[2] = {2, 5};
// Print sum of both.
topics.challenge: Print the first char in char arr[5] = "hello";
topics.terminalNote: Length must be known at compile time.

topics.title: Pointers
topics.explanation: Pointers store memory addresses. Use * to declare, & to get address, * again to dereference.

keyTerms.definition: Variable storing address of another variable.

topics.starterCode:
int x = 10;
// Print address of x
topics.challenge: Change a value via pointer and print the original variable.
topics.terminalNote: Pointers are essential for C data structures and arrays.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Declare return type, name, and arguments. Code modularity and reusability depend on functions in C.
codeExample: int sum(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
topics.starterCode: int triple(int x) { return ; }
commonMistakes: Wrong return type or missing return value.
topics.challenge: Implement a function that checks if an int is positive.
topics.terminalNote: Declare prototype above main if defining below it.

topics.title: Structs
topics.explanation: Structs let you package related data as a single type, with named fields.
codeExample: struct Point { int x; int y; };
topics.starterCode: struct Student { char name[10]; int age; };
solution: struct Student { char name[10]; int age; };
topics.challenge: Declare, initialize, and print a struct with name and year fields.
challengeSolution: struct Book { char name[10]; int year; };
topics.terminalNote: Field access via dot notation.

topics.title: If-Else Statements
topics.explanation: Conditional execution uses if-else statements. Block uses braces, condition is in parentheses.

keyTerms.definition: Performs block if condition is true.

topics.starterCode:
int n = 0;
// Print zero or nonzero
topics.challenge: Test if a number is odd or even.
topics.terminalNote: C convention: 0 false, nonzero true.

topics.title: Loops
topics.explanation: for and while loops repeat execution. For is often used to repeat a fixed number of times.
codeExample: for (int i=0; i<5; i++) { printf("%d\n", i); }
solution: for (int i=0; i<3; i++) { printf("%d\n", i); }
topics.challenge: Sum numbers 1-5 by loop and print.
challengeSolution: for (int i=1; i<=5; i++) sum += i;
topics.terminalNote: Use \n in printf for separate lines.

topics.title: Standard I/O
topics.explanation: Use printf to print, scanf to read. Always match format specifiers with data types.
topics.starterCode: // Print Hello, world!
commonMistakes: Forgetting to use & for int/float in scanf.
topics.challenge: Read an integer and print double its value.
topics.terminalNote: Check standard streams for I/O reliability.

topics.title: Memory Management
topics.explanation: Use malloc and free to allocate and release heap memory. Always free what you malloc!
topics.starterCode: // Allocate space for 2 ints, assign values, free the memory.
topics.challenge: Allocate a char array for a 10-char string, assign and print it, then free.
topics.terminalNote: Heap memory persists after function exit until freed.

name: C++
creator: Bjarne Stroustrup
description: C++ extends C with classes, templates, and more for modern systems, gaming, and performance-intensive applications.
usedFor: Games, desktop applications, performance software, system libraries.
funFact: C++ was first called 'C with Classes.'

topics.title: Variables and Types
topics.explanation: In C++, you must declare the type of each variable. Use int, double, char, std::string, etc.

keyTerms.definition: String class in C++ standard library.

topics.starterCode:
double pi = ;
std::cout << pi;
topics.challenge: Declare std::string s = "C++" and print.
topics.terminalNote: Compile and run the resulting program.

topics.title: Arrays
topics.explanation: Arrays are fixed-size containers for items of a single type.
topics.starterCode:
char letters[4] = {'a','b','c','d'};
// Print last letter
topics.challenge: Sum all elements of int arr[3] = {2, 2, 2}; and print.
topics.terminalNote: Zero-based index.

topics.title: Pointers
topics.explanation: Pointers store addresses, not values. Useful for dynamic memory, arrays, and more.
topics.starterCode: int x=7; int* px=&x; // Print address of x
topics.challenge: Change a value via pointer.
topics.terminalNote: Always initialize pointers before use.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Specify argument and return types for all functions. Use pass-by-reference for modifications.
codeExample: int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }
topics.starterCode: int max(int a, int b) { /* ... */ }
solution: int max(int a, int b) { return a > b ? a : b; }
commonMistakes: Forgetting return value for non-void function.
topics.challenge: Write a function to multiply two doubles.
challengeSolution: double mul(double x, double y){ return x*y; }
topics.terminalNote: Functions declared above main or as prototypes.

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: C++ classes group data and methods, supporting encapsulation and OOP.

keyTerms.definition: A custom object type in C++.

codeExample: class Point { public: int x; int y; };
topics.challenge: Add a constructor to Animal for species.
challengeSolution: class Animal { public: std::string species; Animal(std::string s){species=s;} };
topics.terminalNote: Use public for outside access.

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.explanation: Inheritance allows classes to build on previous ones. Use ': public Base' to inherit.

keyTerms.definition: Acquisition of features from base class.

topics.starterCode: class Base {}; // Derive class Sub
topics.challenge: Override base method in derived class.
challengeSolution: class Base { public: virtual void f(){} };
class D : public Base { public: void f(){} };
topics.terminalNote: Use 'virtual' for correct overriding.

topics.title: Templates
topics.explanation: Templates create code for multiple types. Syntax: template<typename T>.
codeExample: template <typename T> T identity(T x) { return x; }
topics.starterCode: template <typename T> T add(T a, T b) { return ; }
solution: template <typename T> T add(T a, T b) { return a + b; }
topics.challenge: Write a template for min function.
challengeSolution: template<typename T>T min(T a,T b){return a<b?a:b;}
topics.terminalNote: Templates are instantiated at compile time.

topics.title: Standard Template Library (STL)
topics.explanation: STL offers ready-made vectors (dynamic arrays), sets, and maps. Use algorithms like sort, find, etc.
topics.challenge: Create std::map<string, int> and insert pair.
topics.terminalNote: Always include STL headers you use.

topics.title: Memory Management
topics.explanation: Use new/delete for dynamic memory. Modern C++ prefers smart pointers (unique_ptr etc.) to avoid leaks.

keyTerms.definition: C++11 smart pointer with unique ownership semantics.

topics.starterCode:
int* p = new int;
// Assign and print value, then delete.
commonMistakes: Not calling delete on heap memory.
topics.challenge: Create a unique_ptr to array of 2 ints, assign values.
topics.terminalNote: unique_ptr deletes memory when out of scope.

topics.title: Exceptions
topics.explanation: Handle errors with try/catch and use throw to signal an error. Catch by value, reference, or type.
codeExample: try { throw 42; } catch(int e){ std::cout << e; }
topics.starterCode: try { throw "fail"; } catch(...) { }
topics.challenge: Throw and catch a std::string.
challengeSolution: try { throw std::string("Oops"); } catch(const std::string& e){ std::cout << e; }
topics.terminalNote: std exceptions use std::exception base class.

name: C#
creator: Anders Hejlsberg
description: C# is a modern object-oriented language for Windows, .NET, and Unity games.
usedFor: Desktop applications, web services, Unity game development.

topics.title: Variables and Types
topics.explanation: C# variables require type declaration. Popular types: int, double, string, bool.
topics.starterCode:
double d = ;
Console.WriteLine(d);
commonMistakes: Assigning bool to an int variable.
topics.challenge: Declare bool flag = true and print.
topics.terminalNote: C# statements end with semicolon.

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: Define reusable object types and their members using classes. Constructors initialize fields.
topics.challenge: Add a constructor for Book and set title from parameter.
challengeSolution: class Book { public string Title; public Book(string t) { Title = t; } }
topics.terminalNote: Instantiate objects with new.

topics.title: Methods
topics.explanation: Methods are functions inside classes/structs. They can return values and take parameters.
codeExample: public int Add(int x, int y) { return x + y; }
solution: class Greeter { public void Hello() { Console.WriteLine("Hello"); } }
topics.challenge: Write a method Length(string s) returning s.Length.
challengeSolution: public int Length(string s) { return s.Length; }
topics.terminalNote: Methods belong to classes or structs.

topics.title: Properties
topics.explanation: Properties are accessors for fields in a class. They encapsulate get/set functionality.
solution: class Clock { public int Sec { get; set; } }
topics.challenge: Make a read-only property for Name.
topics.terminalNote: Properties go inside class body.

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.explanation: A class can inherit fields and methods from another by using : BaseName.

keyTerms.definition: Acquiring members from a parent class.

codeExample: class Animal { public void Speak() { Console.WriteLine("Hi"); } }
topics.starterCode: class Base { } // Create class Sub : Base
topics.challenge: Override a method in derived class.
challengeSolution: class B { public virtual void Say(){} }
class C:B { public override void Say(){ Console.WriteLine("S"); } }
topics.terminalNote: Derived class can extend/override parents.

topics.title: Interfaces
topics.explanation: An interface defines a contract. A class must implement all its methods.
codeExample: class Tool : IThing { public void Act(){ } }
topics.starterCode:
interface I { void A(); }
class X : I { }
solution: class X : I { public void A(){} }
topics.challenge: Make interface with two methods, implement both.
topics.challengeHint: interface IR { void B(); void C(); }
challengeSolution: interface IR { void B(); void C(); }
class T : IR { public void B(){} public void C(){} }
topics.terminalNote: Multiple interfaces allowed per class.

topics.title: Collections
topics.explanation: The .NET collection types let you store lists, sets, and maps. List<T> is a dynamic array.
topics.starterCode:
List<string> colors = new List<string>();
// Add a color and print it.
topics.challenge: Add 3 ints to a List and print their sum.
topics.terminalNote: System.Linq needed for some operations.

topics.title: Exception Handling
topics.explanation: try/catch handles runtime errors safely. Specific exceptions can be caught.
topics.starterCode: try { /* bad code */ } catch { Console.WriteLine("error"); }
topics.challenge: Catch and print DivideByZeroException.
topics.terminalNote: Detail errors for safer code.

topics.title: LINQ
topics.explanation: LINQ gives SQL-like queries inside C#. Use for filtering, mapping, and aggregate calculations.
topics.starterCode:
int[] vals = {4,5,6};
// Print sum using LINQ
topics.challenge: Print number of elements > 3.
topics.terminalNote: LINQ makes data queries easy and safe.

name: Rust
creator: Graydon Hoare
description: Rust is a modern systems language focused on safety, zero-cost abstractions, and fearless concurrency.
usedFor: System programming, CLI tools, web, embedded systems.
funFact: Rust's mascot is a friendly crab named Ferris.

topics.title: Variables and Mutability
topics.explanation: Rust variables are immutable by default. Use let for assignment, add 'mut' to make them mutable.

keyTerms.definition: Allows the variable to be changed.

topics.starterCode:
let x = 10;
// Make x mutable and change it to 20.
commonMistakes: Trying to change an immutable variable.
topics.challenge: Declare two variables, print their sum.
challengeSolution: let a = 1; let b = 2; println!("{}", a+b);
topics.terminalNote: Every statement needs a semicolon.

topics.title: Ownership
topics.explanation: In Rust, each value has one owner responsible for dropping it; moves transfer ownership.

keyTerms.definition: The variable responsible for cleaning up the value.

codeExample: let t = s; // s is now invalid
topics.starterCode:
let s = String::from("a");
let t = s;
commonMistakes: Using a variable after it's been moved.
Not cloning if you need both.
topics.challenge: Clone the String instead of moving.
topics.terminalNote: Clone is explicit for heap-allocated data.

topics.title: Borrowing and References
topics.explanation: References let you access values without taking ownership. Immutable: &T, mutable: &mut T.
topics.starterCode:
let mut n = 0;
add_one(&mut n);
commonMistakes: Having two mutable borrows at once.
topics.challenge: Write a function that takes an immutable ref and prints the value.
topics.terminalNote: Rust guarantees ref safety at compile time.

topics.title: Structs
topics.explanation: Structs group related data under a single type. Use dot notation to access fields.
codeExample: struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
topics.starterCode: struct Book { title: String, pages: i32 }
solution: struct Book { title: String, pages: i32 }
commonMistakes: Not providing values for all fields.
topics.challenge: Add show() method to Book to print both fields.
challengeSolution: impl Book { fn show(&self){ println!("{} {}", self.title, self.pages); } }
topics.terminalNote: Methods/impl after struct definition.

topics.title: Enums
topics.explanation: Enums define types with a set of named variants, often used for state or pattern matching.
topics.starterCode: enum Light { On, Off }
topics.challenge: Print a string depending on enum value.
challengeSolution: match l { Light::On => println!("On"), Light::Off => println!("Off") }
topics.terminalNote: Pattern match all enum variants.

topics.title: Pattern Matching
topics.explanation: match lets you run code based on value patterns, including destructuring and guards.

keyTerms.definition: Branch based on value and pattern.

topics.starterCode:
let b = true;
match b { /* ... */ }
solution: match b { true => println!("T"), false => println!("F") }
topics.challenge: Pattern match on Option<i32>.
challengeSolution: match x { Some(n) => println!("{}", n), None => println!("empty") }
topics.terminalNote: match enforces exhaustive cases.

topics.title: Traits
topics.explanation: Traits let you define shared methods. Types implement a trait to promise the methods exist.
codeExample: impl Speak for Robot { fn speak(&self){ println!("Beep"); } }
topics.starterCode: trait MyTrait { fn act(&self); }
commonMistakes: Not implementing all methods for the trait.
topics.challenge: Provide default impl for trait method.
topics.terminalNote: Traits enable polymorphism.

topics.title: Generics
topics.explanation: Generics allow code to be written for any type. The <T> syntax defines a type parameter.

keyTerms.definition: A stand-in for a type chosen at use.

topics.starterCode: fn first<T>(arr: &[T]) -> &T { // ... }
commonMistakes: Writing code that is not generic-safe.
topics.challenge: Make a generic struct Pair<T> { first, second }
challengeSolution: struct Pair<T> { first: T, second: T }
topics.terminalNote: Type chosen when code is used.

topics.title: Error Handling
topics.explanation: Rust uses Result types, not exceptions. The ? operator propagates errors up the call stack if not handled.

keyTerms.definition: Enum for Ok(T) or Err(E) outcome.

codeExample: fn div(a: i32, b:i32) -> Result<i32,&'static str>{
topics.starterCode: fn parse(s:&str) -> Result<i32, String> {}
topics.challenge: Return Ok for even, Err for odd integer input.
topics.terminalNote: Use ? to propagate Result errors.

topics.title: Concurrency
topics.explanation: Rust makes safe concurrency possible via threads, channels, and strict ownership rules. spawn runs new threads.
topics.starterCode:
use std::thread;
thread::spawn(|| println!("!"));
commonMistakes: Forgetting to join or handle thread output.
topics.challenge: Spawn two threads, print 'a' and 'b' from each.
topics.terminalNote: Threads may execute in any order.

name: Go
creator: Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, Ken Thompson
description: Go is a simple, statically typed language with lightweight concurrency and great tool support.
usedFor: Cloud servers, web services, CLI tools, microservices.
funFact: Go compiles extremely fast and has a built-in code formatter.

topics.title: Variables and Types
topics.explanation: Use var for explicit type, := for inferred. Go has strict types.
topics.starterCode:
score := 12
// Print double the score
topics.challenge: Create int age=20 and print.
topics.terminalNote: All variables must be used or the compiler will error.

topics.title: Functions
topics.explanation: Define functions with func. Return types go after param list.
codeExample: func sum(a int, b int) int { return a + b }
topics.starterCode:
func hi(name string) string { return "" }
fmt.Println(hi("Sam"))
solution: func hi(name string) string { return "Hi " + name }
topics.challenge: Write a function for double(n int) int.
challengeSolution: func double(n int) int { return n*2 }
topics.terminalNote: Functions are called as in C.

topics.title: Arrays and Slices
topics.explanation: Arrays have fixed size, slices are dynamic. Prefer slices for most work.
topics.starterCode:
letters := []string{"a", "b"}
// Print first
topics.challenge: Append 'c' and print all.
topics.terminalNote: Slices return new slice from append.

topics.title: Structs
topics.explanation: Structs group fields. Go structs do not support methods inside the type itself, but methods can have struct receivers.
topics.starterCode:
type User struct { ID int }
// Print ID for User{5}
topics.challenge: Add Age field to User and print user.Age.
challengeSolution: type User struct{ ID int; Age int }
topics.terminalNote: New types always have capitalized field names for export.

topics.title: Interfaces
topics.explanation: Interfaces define a set of methods. Any type implementing them is considered that interface.
topics.starterCode:
type Item interface{ Print() }
// Implement Print() for struct.
commonMistakes: Method receiver not on concrete type.
topics.challenge: Make an interface with two methods and implement.
topics.terminalNote: No implements keyword — implicit implementation.

topics.title: Goroutines
topics.explanation: Goroutines are lightweight concurrent functions, created by the 'go' keyword.
topics.starterCode: // Launch goroutine to print 'done'
commonMistakes: Exiting program before goroutine runs. Use time.Sleep to see output.
topics.challenge: Launch two goroutines to print 1 and 2.
topics.terminalNote: No ordering guarantee — concurrent!

topics.title: Channels
topics.explanation: Channels are conduits for communicating between goroutines. Use make(chan T) to create.
topics.starterCode:
ch := make(chan string)
// Send 'hi' and print received value
commonMistakes: Not synchronizing both send and receive.
topics.challenge: Send two values, receive both.
topics.terminalNote: Channels block until both sides are ready.

topics.title: Error Handling
topics.explanation: Go does not have exceptions; error is returned as a separate value in the return signature.
topics.starterCode:
f, err := os.Open("foo.txt");
// Check if err != nil
topics.challenge: Return an error from a function when n < 0.
challengeSolution: func foo(n int) error { if n<0 { return errors.New("bad") }; return nil }
topics.terminalNote: Always check error return values!

topics.title: Packages
topics.explanation: Go code is organized into packages. Import packages using import statement.
topics.starterCode:
import "math"
// Print Pi
commonMistakes: Not capitalizing package name in import.
topics.challenge: Import time package and print current time.
topics.terminalNote: Standard library is extensive in Go.

topics.title: HTTP Server
topics.explanation: Go's net/http makes web servers easy to write. Use HandleFunc and ListenAndServe.
topics.starterCode: // Serve /foo path to print 'bar'
topics.challenge: Serve '/hi' and respond with 'hello'.
topics.terminalNote: Test from browser: http://localhost:8080/hi

name: Java
creator: James Gosling
description: Java is an object-oriented, platform-independent language used for stable, maintainable large-scale applications.
funFact: The Java mascot 'Duke' originally waved a flag.

topics.title: Variables and Data Types
topics.explanation: Each Java variable must have a type (int, double, boolean, String, etc.). Type errors are caught at compile time.
topics.starterCode:
double pi = ;
System.out.println(pi);
topics.challenge: Declare boolean ready = true and print.
topics.terminalNote: Variable types are explicit in Java.

topics.title: Arrays
topics.explanation: Arrays are fixed-size collections of a type. Indexing starts at 0.
topics.starterCode:
String[] sa = {"apple", "banana"};
// Print array length.
topics.challenge: Assign new value to second element and print array.
topics.terminalNote: Array types can't grow after creation.

topics.title: Methods
topics.explanation: Methods are functions attached to classes. Use static if they don't need a class instance.

keyTerms.definition: A named code block in a class.

codeExample: static int add(int x, int y){ return x+y; }
topics.starterCode: static int cube(int n){ /* ... */ }
solution: static int cube(int n){ return n*n*n; }
commonMistakes: Using method before definition in the file.
topics.challenge: Write method greet returning "Hello" + param.
challengeSolution: static String greet(String name){ return "Hello " + name; }
topics.terminalNote: Static methods are called via Class.method().

topics.title: Classes
topics.explanation: A class defines the properties and behaviors of objects. Fields and methods go inside.
topics.starterCode: class Car { int year; String model; }
solution: class Car { int year; String model; }
commonMistakes: Forgetting curly braces around class contents.
topics.challenge: Add a constructor to set year and model.
challengeSolution: Car(int y, String m){ year = y; model = m; }
topics.terminalNote: Filename must match public class name.

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.explanation: One class can extend another, inheriting all fields and methods. Use 'extends' keyword.

keyTerms.definition: Extending a class for code reuse.

topics.starterCode: class Parent { } // Declare Child that inherits
topics.challenge: Override a parent method in Child.
challengeSolution: class B extends A { @Override void say(){ System.out.println("b"); } }
topics.terminalNote: @Override tells compiler you're intending to override.

topics.title: Interfaces
topics.explanation: Interfaces define required methods for a class to provide. No bodies in interface.
codeExample: class Demo implements IShow { public void show(){} }
topics.starterCode:
interface Fruit { void eat(); }
class Apple implements Fruit { }
solution: class Apple implements Fruit { public void eat(){} }
topics.challenge: Declare interface X with two methods, and implement.
challengeSolution: interface X { void a(); void b(); }
class Y implements X { public void a(){} public void b(){} }
topics.terminalNote: A class can implement multiple interfaces.

topics.title: Generics
topics.explanation: Generics provide type safety and reduce code duplication. Use angle brackets for parameterized types.
topics.starterCode: class Pair<T> { T first; T second; }
solution: class Pair<T> { T first; T second; }
commonMistakes: Forgetting the <T> in class or method definition.
topics.challenge: Write method <T> head(T[] arr) returns arr[0]
challengeSolution: static <T> T head(T[] arr){ return arr[0]; }
topics.terminalNote: Use generics for flexible data structures.

topics.title: Collections
topics.explanation: Java Collections Framework offers ArrayList, HashMap, etc., that grow dynamically and offer advanced operations.
topics.starterCode:
HashMap<String,Integer> m = new HashMap<>();
// Add a pair and print value.
topics.challenge: Add three elements to list and print sum.
topics.challengeHint: add ints, then loop or use List sum.
topics.terminalNote: ArrayList grows automatically.

topics.title: Exception Handling
topics.explanation: Java uses try/catch/finally constructs for safe error handling at runtime.

keyTerms.definition: Runs code that may cause error.

codeExample: try { int a = 1/0; } catch(Exception e) { System.out.println("fail"); }
topics.starterCode: try { /* ... */ } catch(Exception e) { /* ... */ }
solution: try { int[] a = new int[2]; a[3]=10; } catch(Exception e){ System.out.println("oops"); }
topics.challenge: Catch NumberFormatException and print 'bad'.
topics.terminalNote: Exception hierarchy can be deep!

topics.title: Threads
topics.explanation: Java threads allow concurrent execution. Extend Thread or implement Runnable, and start().
codeExample: class MyThread extends Thread { public void run(){ System.out.println("hello"); } }
topics.starterCode: class T extends Thread { public void run(){ } }
solution: class T extends Thread { public void run(){ System.out.println("T"); } }
commonMistakes: Forgetting to call start() instead of run().
topics.challenge: Create two threads (Runnable) that each print a message.
topics.terminalNote: Thread output order is not guaranteed.
